title: Anaphalioides keriensis
intro: Anaphalioides keriensis is a shrub-like plant species native to New Zealand, belonging to the daisy family (Asteraceae). The leaves and flowers of Anaphalioides keriensis have been used in traditional Maori medicine for their health benefits, including anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties, which are believed to aid in wound healing and reduce fever. Bioactive constituents of this plant include sesquiterpene lactones and triterpenoid saponins, which have been reported to exhibit anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activities. In traditional Maori medicine, Anaphalioides keriensis is often prepared as a tea or infused in hot water, and is also used in topical applications, such as poultices and ointments, to harness its medicinal properties.
taxonomy: Anaphalioides keriensis is a member of the plant family Asteraceae, commonly known as the daisy or sunflower family. This family comprises over 23,000 known species, making it one of the largest and most diverse plant families globally. Anaphalioides keriensis is classified within the genus Anaphalioides, which is a group of flowering plants native to New Zealand. The genus Anaphalioides is further classified within the tribe Gnaphalieae, which contains a variety of daisy-like plants. Anaphalioides keriensis is a unique species within this classification, found specifically in the Kermadec Islands and other parts of New Zealand.
